title: Dynamic Moiré Potentials and Robust Wigner Crystallization in Large-Scale Twisted Transition Metal Dichalcogenides
authors: Yifan Ke, Chuanjing Zeng, Xinming Qin, Wei-Lin Tu, Wei Hu, Jinglong Yang
abstract: Understanding the dynamical evolution of large-scale moiré systems is crucial for connecting theoretical predictions with experimental observations. Here we develop a machine-learning-based workflow, integrating DeePMD and DeepH frameworks with first-principles calculations, to efficiently investigate time-dependent structural and electronic responses in twisted bilayer transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) with experimentally relevant moiré supercells containing over 3000 atoms. Using $\mathrm{WS_2}$ as a representative system, we show that low-temperature lattice vibrations and relaxation deepen the moiré potential wells, narrow the lowest conduction band, and facilitate the formation of strongly localized electronic states. Based on DFT-derived moiré potentials that incorporate these dynamical effects, density-matrix-renormalization-group (DMRG) simulations reveal robust Wigner crystallization and a kagomé-patterned three-electron state, consistent with recent experimental observations. Our workflow provides a practical route for exploring large moiré supercells beyond static configurations and offers new insight into the interplay between lattice dynamics, electronic localization, and emergent correlated states in twisted two-dimensional materials.
summary: This paper presents a new machine-learning workflow to simulate large-scale moire supercells that are too large for traditional DFT. By incorporating lattice vibrations and structural relaxation, the authors show that the moire potential wells deepen, which facilitates the formation of robust Wigner crystals. They specifically identify a kagome-patterned electron state at certain doping levels. This approach provides a practical way to connect atomic-scale dynamics to large-scale emergent electronic phenomena in 2D materials.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between theoretical predictions and experimental observations in large-scale moire systems by accounting for how lattice dynamics and thermal fluctuations influence electronic localization and Wigner crystallization.
main_result: The inclusion of dynamical effects from lattice vibrations deepens moire potential wells and narrows the conduction band, leading to robust Wigner crystallization and the emergence of a kagome-patterned three-electron state.
method: A machine-learning-based workflow integrating DeePMD for molecular dynamics and DeepH for machine-learned electronic Hamiltonians, combined with DFT-level accuracy (HONPAS) and DMRG simulations.
model_or_system: Twisted bilayer tungsten disulfide (TBWS2) at small twist angles (e.g., 2.28 and 7.34 degrees), modeled using an effective Hubbard model with site-dependent potentials and Coulomb interactions.
key_observables: Interlayer distance (d_int), conduction band bandwidth, Local Density of States (LDOS), and electron density distributions.
important_parameters: Twist angles (2.28 to 13.17 degrees), Hubbard U (up to 50 eV), intersite Coulomb interaction V, and temperature (100 K).
main_assumptions: The use of a 11-orbital tight-binding model and the assumption that machine-learned potentials accurately capture the essential physics of the large-scale MD trajectories.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the workflow and structural motifs; Figure 2 illustrates interlayer distance evolution; Figure 4 shows conduction bandwidth; Supplemental figures compare DFT packages (VASP vs HONPAS) and band structures.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a machine-learning workflow, validates its accuracy against plane-wave DFT, applies it to simulate large-scale MD for twisted TMDs, analyzes the resulting electronic structure and band narrowing, and concludes with many-body DMRG simulations of Wigner crystal formation.
why_interesting: The paper demonstrates how structural fluctuations and lattice dynamics can be used to tune many-body phases and emergent geometries (like kagome patterns) in a controlled, theoretically predictable way.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
